title: Typed Lifecycle Policy, Archive Restore, and Cost Modeling · Inference Brief: Retention Policy Conflict
summary: Predict unknown input, typed state, command, stream, promise, storage, cache, security, cost, and recovery outcomes before reviewing lifecycle and storage-cost model; use validated lifecycle configuration, temporal state unions, archive restore polling, retention conflicts, and executable cost models. objectives: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, resolve lifecycle expiration against versioning, Object Lock, legal hold, replication, business retention, and deletion evidence.
In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, request, monitor, authorize, and communicate temporary archive restoration with tier, expiry, duplicate request, and failure states.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Predict Retention Policy Conflict · Theory Retention Policy Conflict
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 7216 during a browser upload compatibility rollout. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to evaluate lifecycle and archive state with retention and minimum-duration cost; separate deterministic browser evidence from controlled Node and AWS transfer gates; then double concurrent Upload instances and preserve aggregate memory and worker budgets. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Retention Policy Conflict contract for the supplied data.
